does: This paper presents Persona-Conditioned Adversarial Prompting (PCAP), an automated red-teaming system that conditions adversarial prompt search on specific attacker personas and strategy cards to discover jailbreaks. The method runs parallel beam searches across different personas, each exploring distinct attack angles and framings. The core contribution is demonstrating that grounding adversarial search in identity-specific contexts and multi-turn tactics substantially improves both attack success rates and the diversity of discovered adversarial prompts. On GPT-OSS~120B, the approach increases attack success rate from approximately 58% to 97%, revealing significantly more attack strategies than identity-agnostic baselines.
connects: This work extends the growing body of adversarial red-teaming research that aims to systematically expose vulnerabilities in large language models before deployment. It builds on prior automated jailbreaking pipelines that use search-based methods to discover adversarial prompts, but identifies and addresses a critical limitation: existing approaches treat attack discovery as identity-neutral, missing attack vectors that depend on persona adoption, social engineering, or multi-turn conversation dynamics. The paper responds to evidence that real-world attacks often leverage specific framings and attacker identities, connecting to research on prompt injection, social engineering vulnerabilities in AI systems, and the importance of diverse threat modeling for AI safety.
enables: By demonstrating that persona-conditioning substantially improves attack discovery, this work enables more comprehensive vulnerability assessment and stress-testing of language models. The modular design, where PCAP is orthogonal to the underlying search algorithm, opens paths for integration with stronger search methods as they develop. The finding that diverse personas uncover different attack strategies suggests future research into persona-specific defenses, more nuanced alignment techniques that account for identity-dependent vulnerabilities, and improved red-teaming benchmarks that explicitly test robustness across multiple attacker contexts. This foundation could inform development of more adversarially robust systems and better threat modeling practices for deployed AI systems.
